title: Reinforcement Learning Assisted Quantum Simulation of Many-Body Excited States and Real-Time Dynamics
authors: Jiaji Zhang, Lipeng Chen, Carlos L. Benavides-Riveros
abstract: The computation of electronic excited states and real-time quantum dynamics of many-fermion systems is among the most promising applications of near-term quantum computing. In this work, we generalize the reinforcement learning contracted quantum eigensolver (RL-CQE), previously developed for ground-state problems, to electronic excited states and real-time quantum dynamics, in which a deep Q-network agent adaptively selects the two-body operators at each iteration, yielding more compact ansätze and improved robustness with respect to critical hyperparameters. A key feature of the algorithm is a scalable state representation based on the ACSE residuals, whose dimension grows with the one-particle basis but remains independent of the number of targeted excited states. We also verify the equivalence of sign-free qubit operators in the excited-state setting, extending a result previously established for ground-state problems. Our RL-CQE for time evolution derives from a constant-scaling ansatz that represents the wave function with a fixed number of unitary transformations independent of simulation time $t$, enabled by the shared unitary structure of the purified ensemble treatment of excited states. Benchmarks on chemical systems demonstrate chemical accuracy with minimal operator counts across a range of bond lengths.
summary: This paper introduces an RL-assisted method to find electronic excited states and simulate real-time dynamics on quantum computers. By using a reinforcement learning agent to pick the best operators, the method creates very efficient, short circuits that do not grow in complexity even as simulation time increases. It demonstrates high accuracy for small molecules like H2 and H3+.
main_problem: Efficiently computing electronic excited states and real-time quantum dynamics of many-fermion systems on near-term quantum devices while overcoming the unbounded growth of circuit depth and the complexity of multi-configurational states.
main_result: The RL-CQE algorithm successfully generalizes to excited states and time evolution, achieving chemical accuracy with highly compact ansätze (minimal operator counts) and a constant-scaling circuit depth for time evolution.
method: A Reinforcement Learning Contracted Quantum Eigensolver (RL-CQE) using a Deep Q-Network (DQN) agent to adaptively select two-body operators based on ACSE residuals, utilizing a purified ensemble approach for excited states.
model_or_system: Electronic systems, specifically the H2 and linear H3+ molecules, modeled with standard electronic Hamiltonians (one-electron and two-electron integrals) mapped to qubits via Jordan-Wigner transformation.
key_observables: Energy eigenvalues, ACSE residual norm, time-dependent fidelity, and potential energy curves.
important_parameters: Ensemble weight vectors, simulation time (t), bond lengths (0.5 to 3.0 Angstroms), and the number of unitary transformations (steps).
main_assumptions: The use of a purified ensemble approach treats excited states as an effective ground-state problem on an enlarged Hilbert space; the algorithm assumes the availability of a one-particle basis.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows potential energy curves for H2; Figure 2 displays the rapid drop of the ACSE residual norm; Figure 3 compares energy eigenvalues under different ensemble weight vectors; Figure 4 shows potential energy curves for H3+ and residual convergence.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of excited-state/dynamic simulation, describes the RL-CQE framework and DQN architecture, details the implementation for time evolution and ensemble weighting, presents benchmarks on H2 and H3+, and concludes with discussion on scalability and limitations.
why_interesting: It presents a significant advancement in quantum algorithms by using RL to automate ansatz construction, specifically solving the scalability issue of circuit depth growth during real-time dynamics, which is crucial for simulating many-body dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
